title: A resolution expressing the sense of the Senate that the United States should recognize the 1994 genocide in Rwanda as "the genocide against the Tutsi in Rwanda".
plain_summary: This Senate resolution wants the United States to use a more specific name when talking about the 1994 genocide in Rwanda. Instead of just calling it the genocide in Rwanda, they want to call it the genocide against the Tutsi in Rwanda. This matches what the United Nations and other countries already call it. The resolution also recognizes that other groups like Hutus and the Twa people were also hurt during that time period.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Foreign Relations. (text: CR S2097-2098)

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution expresses the opinion of the Senate that the United States should recognize the nineteen ninety-four genocide in Rwanda as the genocide against the Tutsi in Rwanda.

sections.title: Background Information
sections.text: In two thousand eighteen, the United Nations General Assembly changed the name of the yearly remembrance day on April seventh to be called the International Day of Reflection on the nineteen ninety-four Genocide against the Tutsi in Rwanda. United States government officials have said publicly that the genocide in Rwanda was meant to destroy the Tutsi people. On April seventh, two thousand twenty-three, Secretary of State Blinken said that the United States stands with Rwanda in remembering the Tutsi victims of genocide and also mourns others who were murdered for opposing the genocidal government. The United States official country plan for Rwanda from March fourteenth, two thousand twenty-two, calls it the nineteen ninety-four genocide against the Tutsi ethnic group. Rwandan officials who fight against denying or changing the history of the genocide say that any name that does not use the exact words genocide against the Tutsi is unclear and helps people deny or change what really happened. The United States Holocaust Memorial Museum says in its teaching materials that the victims were mostly Tutsi and that Hutu extremist leaders wanted all Rwandan Tutsis to be killed. The United States organization Human Rights Watch, which documented the genocide and helped bring criminals to justice, found that the Tutsi were being targeted to be wiped out completely. European diplomats use the term genocide against the Tutsi in their public statements. Starting in two thousand twenty-two, the Government of the United Kingdom has used the words genocide against the Tutsi. The United States is the only major country in the world that publicly refuses to use the term genocide against the Tutsi.

sections.title: Senate Resolution
sections.text: Therefore, the Senate believes that the United States should recognize the nineteen ninety-four genocide in Rwanda as the genocide against the Tutsi in Rwanda. The Secretary of State should publicly confirm this terminology. Other types of terrible acts happened at the same time as the genocide against the Tutsi. The history of the genocide should clearly recognize the other experiences of mass violence against Rwandans during the same time period, including the killings and other violence experienced by Hutus and the Indigenous Twa community, carried out by Hutu extremist militias.
